title: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies
section: Disorders of the Joints and Adjacent Tissues

items.title: Importance of Synovial Fluid Analysis for Differentiating Crystal-Induced Arthropathies
items.content: Synovial fluid analysis using polarizing light microscopy is crucial to distinguish between different types of crystal-induced arthropathies, such as gout and pseudogout. This differentiation is essential due to the distinct therapeutic implications associated with each type.
keyPoints: Polarizing light microscopy can identify most typical crystals involved in arthritis, except for apatite.
Synovial fluid analysis helps distinguish between monosodium urate (MSU), calcium pyrophosphate (CPP), and other crystals.
Differentiating crystal types is important due to their distinct therapeutic implications.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Gout and Other Crystal-Associated Arthropathies

items.title: Mechanism of Acute Inflammation in Crystal-Induced Arthropathies
items.content: Acute inflammation in crystal-induced arthropathies, such as gout or pseudogout, is driven by the shedding of crystals from inert deposits. This process activates the inflammasome and leads to the release of potent proinflammatory cytokines like interleukin (IL) 1 $ \beta $.
keyPoints: Crystal shedding from deposits triggers acute inflammation episodes in gout or pseudogout.
Activation of the inflammasome is a key process behind this inflammation.
Proinflammatory cytokine IL-1 $ \beta $ plays a significant role in the inflammatory response.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Gout and Other Crystal-Associated Arthropathies

items.title: Mechanism of Hyperuricemia and Gout Development
items.content: Gout arises from hyperuricemia, characterized by elevated urate levels due to impaired excretion (90%) or overproduction (10%). This condition leads to monosodium urate (MSU) crystal formation in joints, triggering inflammatory responses.
keyPoints: Hyperuricemia is a prerequisite for gout development.
Impaired urate excretion accounts for 90% of hyperuricemia cases; overproduction accounts for 10%.
MSU crystals form due to urate supersaturation in joints, causing inflammation.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Role of Urate Transporters in Renal Excretion
items.content: Renal excretion is the primary pathway for uric acid elimination, involving specific transporters. URAT1 and OAT10 facilitate apical reabsorption, while GLUT9 mediates basolateral secretion. ABCG2 also plays a role in both renal and intestinal urate handling.
keyPoints: Two-thirds of daily uric acid is eliminated renally; one-third via the gastrointestinal tract.
URAT1 and OAT10 are involved in apical reabsorption; GLUT9 handles basolateral secretion.
ABCG2 transporter plays a role in both renal excretion and intestinal handling.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Inflammatory Response to MSU Crystals in Gout
items.content: MSU crystals provoke an inflammatory response by activating neutrophils and other immune cells, leading to the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines like IL-1, IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-$\alpha$. This process is central to the acute inflammation seen in gout.
keyPoints: MSU crystals stimulate neutrophil activation and respiratory burst.
Inflammation involves cytokines such as IL-1, IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-$\alpha$.
NLRP3 inflammasome plays a role in pro-interleukin synthesis.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.title: Gout Flare Characteristics and Triggers
items.content: Acute gout flares typically affect one joint initially, with the metatarsophalangeal joint of the first toe being involved in 70–90% of cases. These flares often begin at night or early morning, presenting as warm, red, tender, and swollen joints that mimic cellulitis (pseudocellulitis). Triggers include purine-rich foods, alcohol, diuretics, initial urate-lowering therapy, local trauma, and certain medical conditions.
keyPoints: Metatarsophalangeal joint of the first toe involved in 70–90% of cases
Flares often begin at night or early morning
Triggers: purine-rich foods, alcohol, diuretics, initial urate-lowering therapy, local trauma, medical conditions
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Chronic Gout and Genetic Considerations in Women
items.content: Without treatment, chronic gouty arthritis can develop after years of flares, leading to synovitis, subcutaneous tophi, deformity, and bony destruction. Most women with gout are postmenopausal and elderly, often having comorbidities like osteoarthritis, hypertension, or mild renal insufficiency. Genetic mutations in the HPRT gene can lead to hyperuricemia and gout, especially in carrier females who become symptomatic post-menopause.
keyPoints: Chronic gouty arthritis develops after years of untreated flares
Most women with gout are postmenopausal and elderly
HPRT gene mutations linked to hyperuricemia and gout in carrier females
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Confirming Gout Diagnosis: Role of Synovial Fluid Analysis
items.content: Synovial fluid analysis is crucial for confirming a diagnosis of gout, especially when clinical features suggest it. During acute flares, needle-shaped monosodium urate (MSU) crystals are present intracellularly and extracellularly in the synovial fluid. These crystals exhibit bright, negative birefringence under compensated polarized light. The presence of increased leukocytes, ranging from 5,000 to 75,000/µL, contributes to a cloudy appearance of the synovial fluid.
keyPoints: Needle-shaped MSU crystals appear intracellularly and extracellularly during acute gout flares.
MSU crystals show bright, negative birefringence under compensated polarized light.
Synovial fluid leukocyte count ranges from 5,000 to 75,000/µL during acute flares.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Utility of Serum Urate Levels and Imaging in Gout Diagnosis
items.content: While serum urate levels are often elevated at some point in a gout patient's life, they may be normal or low during an acute flare due to the uricosuric effect of inflammatory cytokines like IL-6. Therefore, historical or postflare serum urate values are more useful for diagnosis and guiding therapy. Imaging techniques such as musculoskeletal ultrasound can reveal the double-contour sign indicative of MSU deposition, while dual-energy CT identifies MSU based on its chemical composition.
keyPoints: Serum urate levels may be normal or low during an acute gout flare due to IL-6's uricosuric effect (~2 mg/dL reduction).
Historical or postflare serum urate values are important for diagnosis and therapy targeting.
Musculoskeletal ultrasound can show a double-contour sign over articular cartilage, indicating MSU deposition.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Early Initiation of Anti-Inflammatory Drugs in Acute Gout
items.content: Initiating anti-inflammatory treatment promptly during a gout flare can significantly reduce the severity or abort it. For patients with recurrent flares, having a supply of medications ready to use at the first sign of symptoms is beneficial.
keyPoints: Early initiation of anti-inflammatory drugs helps in reducing the severity of gout flares.
Patients with recurrent flares should have their medications ready for immediate use.
Oral colchicine regimen: 1.2 mg initially, followed by 0.6 mg in 1 hour.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Acute Gout Care

items.title: Choosing Anti-Inflammatory Drugs Based on Patient Factors
items.content: The selection of anti-inflammatory drugs for acute gout management depends on patient-specific factors such as comorbidities, concurrent medications, and previous responses to treatments. Colchicine, NSAIDs, and glucocorticoids are mainstays, with specific dosing adjustments needed in certain populations.
keyPoints: Choice of anti-inflammatory drugs is influenced by comorbidities and concurrent medications.
Colchicine dose adjustment is necessary for patients with renal disease or those on P-glycoprotein/CYP3A4 inhibitors.
NSAIDs dosing: Indomethacin 25–50 mg tid, Naproxen 500 mg bid, Ibuprofen 800 mg tid, Celecoxib initial 800 mg followed by 400 mg after 12 hours then 400 mg bid.
Glucocorticoids can be administered orally (e.g., Prednisone 30–50 mg/d) or via intraarticular injection for localized joint involvement.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Acute Gout Care

items.title: Initiation of Urate-Lowering Therapy in Gout Patients
items.content: Urate-lowering therapy should be considered for gout patients with chronic hyperuricemia, especially when lifestyle modifications are insufficient. Initiation is typically based on the frequency and severity of flares or the presence of tophi or uric acid nephrolithiasis.
keyPoints: Target serum urate level: <300–360 μmol/L (5.0–6.0 mg/dL)
Indications for therapy: two or more gout attacks yearly, severe flares, tophi, chronic arthritis, or nephrolithiasis
Consider patient's willingness for long-term therapy
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · URATE-LOWERING THERAPY

items.title: Allopurinol as First-Line Urate-Lowering Therapy
items.content: Allopurinol, a xanthine oxidase inhibitor, is the first-line treatment for gout. It should be started at low doses and titrated up to achieve target serum urate levels while minimizing risks of adverse reactions.
keyPoints: Starting dose: 100 mg daily or less
Target serum urate level: <5–6 mg/dL
Adjustment for CKD: start at 50 mg if eGFR <30 mL/min
Risk factors for SCARs: high initial doses, CKD, HLA-B*5801 carriage
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · URATE-LOWERING THERAPY

items.title: Managing Urate-Lowering Therapy Initiation During Gout Flares
items.content: Urate-lowering drugs should not be started during active gout flares due to the risk of exacerbation. They can be initiated during or after flare resolution with anti-inflammatory prophylaxis to prevent paradoxical flares.
keyPoints: Avoid initiation during active flares
Use colchicine (0.6 mg one to two times daily) or naproxen (250 mg twice daily) as prophylaxis
Continue prophylaxis until normouriemic and no attacks for 3–6 months or tophi resolve
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · URATE-LOWERING THERAPY

items.title: Age-Related Prevalence of CPPD
items.content: CPP deposition disease (CPPD) predominantly affects the elderly, with a significant increase in prevalence as age advances. The likelihood of CPPD doubles every decade after 60 years, reaching nearly 50% in individuals over 85.
keyPoints: Prevalence of CPPD increases with age, doubling each decade post-60.
Nearly 50% prevalence in those >85 years old.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CALCIUM PYROPHOSPHATE DEPOSITION DISEASE

items.title: Consider Metabolic Disorders in Younger CPPD Patients
items.content: In patients under 50 with CPPD, metabolic abnormalities or hereditary forms should be considered. This includes evaluating for conditions like hyperparathyroidism and hemochromatosis.
keyPoints: CPPD arthritis in individuals <50 years warrants investigation of metabolic disorders.
Conditions to consider include hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, hypophosphatasia, and hypomagnesemia.
Evaluation should include serum calcium, phosphorus, alkaline phosphatase, magnesium, iron, and transferrin.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CALCIUM PYROPHOSPHATE DEPOSITION DISEASE

items.title: Distinguishing Acute CPP Crystal Arthritis from Gout
items.content: Acute CPP crystal arthritis, often termed pseudogout, can mimic gout but has distinct clinical features. It frequently affects the knee and wrist rather than the first metatarsophalangeal joint (podagra), which is rarely involved. Episodes tend to last longer, sometimes weeks to months, with systemic signs like fever and elevated acute-phase reactants.
keyPoints: Knee is most commonly affected joint, followed by wrist; podagra rarely affected.
Initial episodes can last weeks to months.
Systemic signs include fever, chills, and elevated acute-phase reactants.
May be precipitated by trauma, severe illness, or surgery (e.g., parathyroidectomy).
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.title: Chronic CPP Crystal Arthritis Patterns and Diagnostic Clues
items.content: Chronic CPP crystal arthritis often resembles osteoarthritis but affects atypical joints like the metacarpophalangeal, wrist, elbow, shoulder, or ankle. It can present as chronic symmetric synovitis similar to rheumatoid arthritis or severe destructive disease mimicking neuropathic arthritis. Radiographs showing chondrocalcinosis increase diagnostic likelihood.
keyPoints: Mimics osteoarthritis with severe damage in atypical joints (e.g., metacarpophalangeal, wrist).
Can resemble rheumatoid arthritis or mimic neuropathic arthritis radiographically.
Chondrocalcinosis on imaging increases likelihood of CPPD disease.
Definitive diagnosis requires demonstration of rhomboid or rodlike crystals in synovial fluid.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: CPPDDisease | ■ PATHOGENESIS | TABLE 384-3 Clinical Manifestations of Apatite Crystal Deposition | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Management Strategies for Acute and Chronic CPP Crystal Arthritis
items.content: Acute CPP crystal arthritis is managed similarly to gout, using treatments such as ice packs, joint fluid aspiration with glucocorticoid injection, oral colchicine, NSAIDs, systemic glucocorticoids, and IL-1β antagonist anakinra for severe cases. Prophylactic daily colchicine can help prevent frequent recurrent attacks. Chronic CPP crystal arthritis lacks effective methods to remove crystals from cartilage and synovium; however, NSAIDs (with gastric protection if needed), colchicine, low-dose glucocorticoids, hydroxychloroquine, or methotrexate may alleviate chronic synovitis. Progressive destructive arthropathy may necessitate joint replacement.
keyPoints: Anti-inflammatory treatments for acute CPP crystal arthritis include ice packs, joint fluid aspiration with glucocorticoid injection, oral colchicine, NSAIDs, systemic glucocorticoids, and IL-1β antagonist anakinra for severe cases.
Prophylactic daily colchicine can be used to prevent frequent recurrent attacks of CPP crystal arthritis.

Chronic CPP crystal arthritis lacks effective methods to remove crystals from cartilage and synovium; NSAIDs (with gastric protection if needed), colchicine, low-dose glucocorticoids, hydroxychloroquine, or methotrexate may help with chronic synovitis.
Progressive destructive arthropathy in CPP crystal arthritis may require joint replacement surgery.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CPPDDisease | ■ PATHOGENESIS | TABLE 384-3 Clinical Manifestations of Apatite Crystal Deposition | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: CPPDDisease | ■ PATHOGENESIS | TABLE 384-3 Clinical Manifestations of Apatite Crystal Deposition | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Prevalence of Apatite Crystal Arthropathy
items.content: Apatite crystal deposition can lead to periarticular or articular deposits causing acute reversible inflammation or chronic joint damage. Common sites include bursae and tendons around knees, shoulders, hips, and fingers. In elderly patients, apatite aggregates in synovial fluid are associated with a destructive arthropathy known as Milwaukee shoulder, affecting shoulders, hips, knees, and fingers. Joint destruction leads to instability and deformity, progressing indolently from minimal to severe pain and disability, potentially necessitating joint replacement.
keyPoints: Apatite crystal deposition can cause acute reversible inflammation or chronic damage in joints.
Common sites for apatite deposition include bursae and tendons around knees, shoulders, hips, and fingers.
30–50% of patients with osteoarthritis have apatite microcrystals in their synovial fluid.
Apatite aggregates are associated with destructive arthropathy (Milwaukee shoulder) in elderly, affecting shoulders, hips, knees, and fingers.
Joint destruction from apatite deposition leads to instability, deformity, and may require joint replacement.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CPPDDisease | ■ PATHOGENESIS | TABLE 384-3 Clinical Manifestations of Apatite Crystal Deposition | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Apatite Deposition Disease | ■ PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Diagnosis of Apatite Arthropathy: Synovial Fluid Analysis and Crystal Identification
items.content: Apatite arthropathy, also known as basic calcium phosphate disease, is diagnosed by identifying crystals in synovial fluid or tissue. The synovial fluid leukocyte count is typically low (<2000/μL) with a predominance of mononuclear cells despite severe symptoms. Crystals are very small and require electron microscopy for definitive identification.
keyPoints: Synovial fluid leukocyte count in apatite arthritis: usually <2000/μL
Crystals appear as 1- to 20-μm shiny intra- or extracellular nonbirefringent globules or aggregates
Definitive identification of crystals requires electron microscopy with energy-dispersive elemental analysis, x-ray diffraction, infrared spectroscopy, or Raman microspectroscopy
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Apatite Deposition Disease | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Apatite Deposition Disease | ■ PATHOGENESIS
items.title: Pathogenesis of CaOx Arthritis in Chronic Renal Disease
items.content: In chronic renal disease, calcium oxalate (CaOx) deposits are recognized as a cause of arthritis. Patients often depend on long-term hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis and may have received ascorbic acid supplements, which metabolize to oxalate. This can lead to hyperoxalosis due to inadequate clearance in uremia.
keyPoints: CaOx deposits are a known cause of arthritis in chronic renal failure
Patients often on long-term hemodialysis or peritoneal dialysis
Ascorbic acid supplements metabolize to oxalate, which is inadequately cleared in uremia
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Apatite Deposition Disease | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease
items.title: Distinguishing Features of CaOx Arthritis
items.content: CaOx arthritis can present similarly to other crystal arthropathies like MSU, CPP, or apatite. However, radiographs may show chondrocalcinosis or soft tissue calcifications, and synovial effusions are typically noninflammatory with leukocyte counts <2000/μL.
keyPoints: Radiographic findings: chondrocalcinosis or soft tissue calcifications
Synovial fluid analysis: usually noninflammatory with <2000 leukocytes/μL
Crystal characteristics: variable shape, birefringence under polarized light; recognizable forms are bipyramidal with strong birefringence and stain with alizarin red S
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease
items.title: Treatment Approaches for CaOx Arthropathy
items.content: Standard treatments like NSAIDs, colchicine, and intraarticular glucocorticoids provide only slight improvement in CaOx arthropathy. In primary oxalosis, liver transplantation significantly reduces crystal deposits.
keyPoints: Limited efficacy of NSAIDs, colchicine, and intraarticular glucocorticoids
Liver transplantation can significantly reduce crystal deposits in primary oxalosis
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease

items.question: Which technique is NOT sufficient alone for identifying apatite crystals during synovial fluid analysis?
items.explanation: Polarized light microscopy alone cannot identify apatite crystals.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Gout and Other Crystal-Associated Arthropathies

items.question: What process is considered key behind the episodic manifestation of acute inflammation in gout or pseudogout?
items.explanation: Crystal shedding from inert deposits is key to episodic acute inflammation.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Gout and Other Crystal-Associated Arthropathies

items.question: Which cytokine is involved in the inflammatory response triggered by crystal deposition?
items.explanation: Interleukin (IL) 1 $ \beta $ is involved in the inflammatory response.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Gout and Other Crystal-Associated Arthropathies

items.question: Which factor is primarily responsible for the development of hyperuricemia in most cases?
options: Overproduction of urate due to increased cell turnover.
Impaired excretion of urate by the kidneys.
items.explanation: Hyperuricemia arises predominantly from impaired urate excretion (90%).
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What is the primary mechanism of urate reabsorption in the renal proximal tubule?
options: Passive diffusion through the basolateral membrane.
Sodium-dependent uptake at the apical membrane.
items.explanation: Urate reabsorption at the apical membrane of the proximal tubule is sodium-dependent.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which transporter is primarily responsible for urate secretion across the basolateral membrane in the renal proximal tubule?
items.explanation: Urate is excreted across the basolateral membrane via the GLUT9 transporter.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: What role does NLRP3 inflammasome play in gout pathogenesis?
options: It promotes neutrophil chemotaxis and activation.
It triggers the synthesis of pro-inflammatory cytokines.
items.explanation: NLRP3 inflammasome leads to the generation of pro-interleukin (IL)-1 and IL-1 $ \beta $, among other proinflammatory molecules.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.question: Which joint is most commonly affected by early gout flares?
options: Metatarsophalangeal joint of the first toe
items.explanation: The metatarsophalangeal joint of the first toe is involved in 70–90% of early gout cases (podagra).
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What clinical appearance can mimic cellulitis during a gout flare?
items.explanation: The affected joints and periarticular areas can become warm, red, tender, and swollen, often mimicking cellulitis (pseudocellulitis).
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common trigger for gout flares?
items.explanation: Triggers of gout flares include purine-rich food, alcohol, and initial introduction of urate-lowering therapy; a high carbohydrate diet is not mentioned as a trigger.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: Which type of microscopy is used to identify MSU crystals by their birefringence properties?
items.explanation: MSU crystals show bright, negative birefringence under compensated polarized light.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the typical appearance of synovial fluid during an acute gout flare?
items.explanation: Synovial fluid appears cloudy due to the increased numbers of leukocytes during an acute gout flare.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which imaging modality can reveal a double-contour sign indicating MSU deposition?
items.explanation: Musculoskeletal ultrasound can reveal a double-contour sign overlying the articular cartilage, indicating MSU deposition.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which of the following medications used for acute gout care is primarily eliminated through P-glycoprotein and CYP3A4?
items.explanation: Colchicine is eliminated via P-glycoprotein and CYP3A4.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Acute Gout Care

items.question: For which patients should the dose of colchicine be reduced, particularly for prolonged use?
options: Patients using P-glycoprotein or CYP3A4 inhibitors
items.explanation: Colchicine dose is reduced in patients using P-glycoprotein or CYP3A4 inhibitors.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Acute Gout Care

items.question: Which of the following medications can be administered as an intraarticular injection for acute gout?
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids like prednisone can be given as intraarticular injections.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Acute Gout Care

items.question: What is the typical target serum urate level for preventing recurrent gout flares and eliminating tophi?
items.explanation: The target serum urate level for preventing recurrent gout flares and eliminating tophi is typically <300–360 μmol/L.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · URATE-LOWERING THERAPY

items.question: Which xanthine oxidase inhibitor does not require dose adjustment in moderate to severe chronic kidney disease?
items.explanation: Febuxostat is predominantly metabolized by the liver and does not require dose adjustment in moderate to severe chronic kidney disease.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · URATE-LOWERING THERAPY

items.question: Which uricosuric agent is more effective in patients with CKD?
items.explanation: Benzbromarone, although not available in the United States, is more effective than probenecid in patients with CKD.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · URATE-LOWERING THERAPY

items.question: When should urate-lowering drugs generally be initiated in relation to gout flares?
options: At the onset of a gout flare
During the resolution phase or after a gout flare with anti-inflammatory prophylaxis
Only when serum urate levels are normal
items.explanation: Urate-lowering drugs should generally be initiated during the resolution phase of or after a gout flare, together with anti-inflammatory prophylaxis.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · URATE-LOWERING THERAPY

items.question: What is the primary pathogenic process thought to be central in patients with CPPD arthritis?
options: Increased levels of inorganic pyrophosphate in cartilage matrix.
Decreased levels of glycosaminoglycans in cartilage.
items.explanation: Increased levels of inorganic pyrophosphate in the cartilage matrix are central to CPPD arthritis, analogous to hyperuricemia in gout.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CALCIUM PYROPHOSPHATE DEPOSITION DISEASE

items.question: Which gene mutation is associated with increased elaboration and extracellular transport of pyrophosphate?
items.explanation: Mutations in the ANKH gene have been found to increase elaboration and extracellular transport of pyrophosphate.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CALCIUM PYROPHOSPHATE DEPOSITION DISEASE

items.question: Which condition should be considered if CPPD arthritis is present in individuals aged <50 years?
options: Familial and sporadic cases of ANKH mutations.
items.explanation: The presence of CPPD arthritis in individuals aged <50 years should lead to consideration of metabolic disorders or hereditary forms of the disease.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CALCIUM PYROPHOSPHATE DEPOSITION DISEASE

items.question: Which joint is most commonly affected by acute CPP crystal arthritis?
items.explanation: The knee is the joint most commonly affected by acute CPP crystal arthritis.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What condition can precipitate an acute attack of CPP crystal arthritis?
items.explanation: Acute attacks of CPP crystal arthritis may be precipitated by trauma.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.question: What is the typical cell type found predominantly in synovial fluid during an acute CPP crystal attack?
items.explanation: The predominant cell type in synovial fluid during an acute CPP crystal attack is the neutrophil.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.explanation: Apatite, specifically hydroxyapatite, is the primary mineral in bones and teeth.

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease
items.question: Which of the following is a recognized form of CaOx crystals?
items.explanation: The most easily recognized forms of CaOx crystals are bipyramidal, have strong birefringence, and stain with alizarin red S.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease
items.question: Which treatment has shown significant reduction in crystal deposits in primary oxalosis?
items.explanation: In primary oxalosis, liver transplantation has induced a significant reduction in crystal deposits.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease

items.statement: Polarizing light microscopy can identify all types of crystals typically found in synovial fluid.
items.explanation: It cannot identify apatite crystals alone.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Gout and Other Crystal-Associated Arthropathies

items.statement: Synovial fluid analysis is important to distinguish between different types of crystal-induced arthritis.
items.explanation: It helps identify the specific type of crystals involved.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Gout and Other Crystal-Associated Arthropathies

items.statement: Humans are the only mammals known to develop gout spontaneously due to their loss of uricase enzyme.
items.explanation: Humans often develop hyperuricemia with their evolutionary species-wide loss of uricase.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: The majority of daily uric acid elimination occurs via the gastrointestinal tract.
items.explanation: Approximately two-thirds of daily uric acid elimination occurs through renal excretion, not the gastrointestinal tract.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.statement: Most women with gout are premenopausal.
items.explanation: Most women with gout are postmenopausal and elderly.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Genetic mutations in the HPRT gene can lead to Lesch-Nyhan syndrome.
items.explanation: Lesch-Nyhan syndrome is linked to genetic mutations in the HPRT gene, causing severe neurologic and behavioral abnormalities.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Serum urate levels are always elevated during an acute gout flare.
items.explanation: Serum urate levels can be normal or low at the time of an acute flare due to inflammatory cytokines' uricosuric property.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: MSU crystals are typically present both intracellularly and extracellularly during acute gout flares.
items.explanation: During acute gout flares, needle-shaped MSU crystals are typically present both intracellularly and extracellularly.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: NSAIDs are the preferred treatment for acute gout in patients with complicating comorbidities.
items.explanation: NSAIDs are used most often in individuals without complicating comorbidities.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Acute Gout Care

items.statement: Anakinra or canakinumab may be used when other treatments for gout flares have failed.
items.explanation: These medications are useful options when other treatments are contraindicated or have failed.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Acute Gout Care

items.statement: Allopurinol is contraindicated in patients with chronic kidney disease.
items.explanation: Allopurinol can be used in CKD, but the starting dose should be lower and titrated up slowly.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · URATE-LOWERING THERAPY

items.statement: HLA-B $ ^{+} $5801 screening is recommended before starting allopurinol in populations with a high carriage rate.
items.explanation: Screening for HLA-B $ ^{+} $5801 is advised in certain populations to reduce the risk of SCARs when initiating allopurinol.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · URATE-LOWERING THERAPY

items.statement: The prevalence of CPPD increases with age, nearly reaching 50% in those over 85 years old.
items.explanation: Prevalence of CPPD doubles in articular tissues with each decade >60 years of age, nearing 50% for those >85.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CALCIUM PYROPHOSPHATE DEPOSITION DISEASE

items.statement: CPP crystals are deposited primarily due to increased levels of ATP in the cartilage matrix.
items.explanation: CPP crystal deposition is associated with increased levels of inorganic pyrophosphate, not directly with ATP.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CALCIUM PYROPHOSPHATE DEPOSITION DISEASE

items.statement: Chronic CPP crystal arthritis can mimic rheumatoid arthritis.
items.explanation: Chronic symmetric synovitis in CPP crystal arthritis is clinically similar to rheumatoid arthritis.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.statement: Acute gout flares and acute CPP crystal arthritis attacks typically last the same duration.
items.explanation: Initial episodes of acute CPP crystal arthritis tend to last longer than typical gout flares.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: CPPDDisease | ■ PATHOGENESIS | TABLE 384-3 Clinical Manifestations of Apatite Crystal Deposition | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Familial aggregation is commonly seen in apatite crystal deposition diseases.
items.explanation: Familial aggregation is rarely seen in these conditions.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CPPDDisease | ■ PATHOGENESIS | TABLE 384-3 Clinical Manifestations of Apatite Crystal Deposition | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: CPPDDisease | ■ PATHOGENESIS | TABLE 384-3 Clinical Manifestations of Apatite Crystal Deposition | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS
items.statement: Apatite crystals can be identified in clinically stable osteoarthritic joints.
items.explanation: 30–50% of patients with osteoarthritis have apatite microcrystals in their synovial fluid.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CPPDDisease | ■ PATHOGENESIS | TABLE 384-3 Clinical Manifestations of Apatite Crystal Deposition | CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Apatite Deposition Disease | ■ PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: CaOx deposits are recognized as a cause of arthritis in chronic renal failure.
items.explanation: CaOx deposits have been known to be one of the causes of arthritis in patients with chronic renal failure.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Apatite Deposition Disease | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Apatite Deposition Disease | ■ PATHOGENESIS
items.statement: Aspirations and NSAIDs are ineffective for treating acute attacks of bursitis or synovitis.
items.explanation: Aspiration of effusions and the use of NSAIDs can shorten the duration and intensity of symptoms in acute attacks.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Apatite Deposition Disease | ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease
items.statement: CaOx crystals are always noninflammatory.
items.explanation: CaOx-induced synovial effusions can be mildly inflammatory.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease
items.statement: Radiographs may reveal chondrocalcinosis in CaOx deposition disease.
items.explanation: Radiographs may indeed reveal chondrocalcinosis or soft tissue calcifications.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease

items.assertion: Crystal deposits can lead to chronic erosive changes in articular structures.
items.reason: This is due to the physical, inflammatory, and catalytic effects of crystals on cartilage and joint structures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Gout and Other Crystal-Associated Arthropathies

items.assertion: Gout is associated with multiple cardiometabolic-kidney comorbidities.
items.reason: Hyperuricemia and gout are linked to conditions like obesity, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, myocardial infarction, stroke, chronic kidney disease (CKD), and urate nephrolithiasis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the association.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: Inherited metabolic disorders account for most cases of hyperuricemia.
items.reason: A minority of hyperuricemia cases (10%) are due to inherited metabolic disorders like heightened activity of PRPP synthetase or deficits in HPRT.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; a minority, not most, cases of hyperuricemia are due to inherited metabolic disorders.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · ■ PATHOGENESIS

items.assertion: Early initiation of anti-inflammatory drugs helps reduce the severity of gout flares.
items.reason: Anti-inflammatory drugs, such as colchicine and NSAIDs, target pathways involved in inflammation during a gout flare.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · Acute Gout Care

items.assertion: Urate-lowering therapy can be initiated during active gout flares.
items.reason: Initiating urate-lowering drugs during active flares may worsen the flare by acutely lowering serum urate levels.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; urate-lowering therapy should not be initiated during active gout flares to avoid worsening them.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · URATE-LOWERING THERAPY

items.assertion: Febuxostat can be used in patients who carry the HLA-B $ ^{+} $5801 allele.
items.reason: Febuxostat is metabolized by the liver and does not require dose adjustment for CKD, making it suitable for these patients.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; Febuxostat can be used in HLA-B $ ^{+} $5801 carriers due to its metabolism pathway.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · URATE-LOWERING THERAPY

items.subtopic: CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease
items.reason: The most easily recognized forms of CaOx crystals are bipyramidal with strong birefringence.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion as it refers to a specific form.
items.reference: Gout and Other Crystal Associated Arthropathies · CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS AND DIAGNOSIS | Calcium Oxalate Deposition Disease
